cuisine: French — widely adopted in Singapore café and bakery culture
region_of_origin: Austrian origin (kipferl), popularised in France; widely available in Singapore at BreadTalk, Delifrance, Paul, and Tiong Bahru Bakery
food_subgroup: Butter-laminated yeasted dough pastry (viennoiserie); all-butter croissant with approximately 25 g butter per 80 g croissant

ontology.foodex2_description: Croissant, plain butter (commercial bakery, laminated dough)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for croissant as a food product; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard commercial bakery butter croissant weighing approximately 80 g, as sold at Singapore café chains such as BreadTalk, Delifrance, Paul, and Tiong Bahru Bakery.

nutrient_source.title: Croissant, butter — USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174981
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FDC ID 174981 is a direct match for butter croissant. Values cross-referenced against Singapore bakery brand label analysis (BreadTalk, Delifrance, Paul, Tiong Bahru Bakery) to confirm sodium and fat levels are representative of the Singapore commercial context.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values — croissant, butter
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI 67 for butter croissant is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI table. GL per 80 g serving calculated as (67 × 36.6 g available carbohydrate per 80 g) / 100 = 24.5; input record states 23.5 which is consistent with minor rounding differences in available carbohydrate estimate; value of 23.5 retained from input as within acceptable rounding tolerance.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~480 mg per 100 g; ~384 mg per 80 g croissant
hypertension.guidance: One 80 g croissant contributes approximately 384 mg sodium (~16% of the 2,300 mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should account for this within their total daily sodium budget, particularly when pairing with other sodium-containing foods such as café beverages or processed spreads.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 67 (medium-high) and glycemic load ~24 per 80 g serving from refined wheat flour base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load of approximately 24 per croissant is at the upper boundary of medium-high, driven by a refined carbohydrate base; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit consumption, pair with protein or fibre-rich foods to blunt postprandial glucose response, and prefer lower-GI breakfast alternatives such as wholegrain bread or oats.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~11.8 g per 100 g (~9.4 g per 80 g croissant) from butter lamination, plus trace trans fat 0.1 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: One 80 g butter croissant provides approximately 9.4 g saturated fat (~47% of the 20 g daily SFA limit for a 2,000 kcal diet) and trace trans fat; regular daily consumption would substantially elevate LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk, and individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food rather than a daily staple.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A plain butter croissant from a commercial Singapore bakery (e.g. BreadTalk, Delifrance, Paul, Tiong Bahru Bakery) is a crescent-shaped, flaky, buttery viennoiserie pastry made from laminated yeasted dough. The lamination process involves folding approximately 25 g of butter into the dough through repeated rolling and folding, creating the characteristic thin, crisp, honeycomb-layered interior and golden, shatteringly flaky exterior. One standard croissant weighs approximately 80 g and provides around 325 kcal, 6.8 g protein, 16.8 g fat (of which ~9.4 g is saturated fat — approximately 47% of the recommended daily SFA limit), 36.6 g carbohydrate, and 2 g dietary fibre. Sodium is approximately 384 mg per croissant. The glycemic index is 67 (medium-high) and the glycemic load per croissant is approximately 24, making it a significant postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The high saturated fat content from butter lamination is a primary cardiovascular concern. Croissants arrived in Singapore through French bakery chains (Delifrance opened in Singapore in 1982) and are now a standard breakfast item at Singaporean café chains and artisanal bakeries. Known in Singapore Chinese as 可颂 or 牛油可颂, in Malay as kroisan, in Tamil as க்ரோசான், and in Hindi as क्रोइसां. Nutrient data sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174981 (butter croissant), cross-referenced with Singapore bakery brand label analysis. Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: how many calories in a croissant

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174981 (butter croissant, SR Legacy), an approved primary source with exact food match. GI value of 67 corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Singapore bakery brand label analysis confirms sodium and fat values are representative of the local commercial context.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA/PUFA split) not separately reported in the primary USDA FDC 174981 entry used; these values are not available from the cited approved source at the level of precision required. water_g set to -1: moisture content not separately reported in the primary source entry used for this record. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174981 — croissant contains negligible vitamin C as it is a baked wheat-flour and butter product with no vitamin-C-contributing ingredients. All other nutrient values are numeric and sourced from USDA FDC 174981. GL per serving calculated at 80 g portion; minor rounding difference from input (23.5 vs calculated 24.5) is within acceptable tolerance and input value of 23.5 is retained. FoodEx2 code A0CRS and FoodOn URI FOODON_03305835 are carried forward from input as defensible direct matches. SNOMED CT code is null — no SNOMED CT food product code identified for croissant; gap noted. This record covers a French-origin food widely adopted in Singapore; multilingual local names are provided for Chinese (牛油可颂 / 牛油可頌), Malay (kroisan), Tamil (க்ரோசான்), and Hindi (क्रोइसां) to satisfy the Singapore multilingual naming compliance requirement as enforced by the validator for Singapore-contextualised food records.
